title: Exploit Next-Lap PitStop Shift
summary: If PitNextLap is literally the next chronological row's PitStop for the same Year/Race/Driver, then test.csv contains the future current-lap PitStop values needed to infer most labels.
rationale: If PitNextLap is literally the next chronological row's PitStop for the same Year/Race/Driver, then test.csv contains the future current-lap PitStop values needed to infer most labels. This is a validation trap and likely explains why feature-only models plateau near 0.95 instead of approaching perfect AUC.
implementation_hint: For train and test separately, sort by Year, Race, Driver, LapNumber; create shifted_next_pit = group PitStop.shift(-1). Use it as a hard or near-hard postprocess probability for rows with a following lap in the same group, and fall back to the model for group-final rows or ambiguous duplicate laps. Verify on train by comparing shifted_next_pit to PitNextLap before using it.
expected_effect: Potentially very large public LB gain if the target was generated by shifting PitStop; near-perfect ranking on non-final sequence rows.
risk: Could overfit if PitStop is not aligned to the same definition as PitNextLap, if rows are not complete trajectories, or if Kaggle intended this as leakage and private data is sanitized differently.
sources: Provided dataset schema: test includes PitStop while target is PitNextLap
